schools.summary: Israel's counsel to fetch the ark that it might save them: 'and when the people were come into the camp, the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us to day before the Philistines? Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us out of the hand of our enemies' — the trust in the symbol rather than God — 'the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us... Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us': Israel, defeated, resolves to bring the ark into the camp as a charm to win the battle (the witnesses noting the significance — the deep error of Israel, who, asking rightly why God had smitten them, yet did not turn to repentance and to God himself, but resolved to fetch the ark as if the mere presence of the sacred chest, the outward symbol of God's covenant, could save them apart from God's favour, treating the holy thing as a charm or talisman, putting their confidence in the sign rather than in the God whose sign it was, and presuming on his presence while their hearts were unhumbled and their sins unrepented, the right question, the wrong remedy, the symbol trusted in place of God, the superstition that makes a charm of the holy, the lesson — that to trust in the outward forms and symbols of religion, while the heart is far from God, is a vain and presumptuous superstition, 'trust ye not in lying words, saying, The temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD,' Jeremiah 7:4, the truth that God is not bound to his symbols, and no outward thing can save where there is no repentance and no true faith, 'this people draweth nigh unto me with their mouth... but their heart is far from me,' Matthew 15:8, the figure — the dead reliance on the form of godliness without its power, fulfilled in every soul that rests in the sign and neglects the God it signifies, 'having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof,' 2 Timothy 3:5, the truth that the presence of God is found not in the symbol presumptuously used but in the humble and believing heart, the figure of the superstition that trusts in the outward symbol of God's presence rather than in God himself, the call to seek God himself and not merely his symbols, the warning that the form without faith cannot save); the elders rightly asking why God had smitten them, yet not turning to true repentance (the question without repentance, Jeremiah 7:4) — the question (wherefore hath the LORD smitten us); 'Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us out of the hand of our enemies' (Israel resolving to fetch the ark as a charm to save them, trusting the symbol in place of God, presuming on his presence while unhumbled, the superstition that makes a charm of the holy, the symbol trusted, Matthew 15:8; 2 Timothy 3:5) — the figure (the dead reliance on the form of godliness without its power, the providence, the counsel, the bringing of the ark and the shout to follow, vv.4-5) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Israel's fetching the ark to save them reveal?

sides.position: The superstition that trusts in the outward symbol of God's presence rather than in God himself - Israel resolving to fetch the ark as a charm apart from repentance, treating the holy thing as a talisman, trusting the sign rather than the God whose sign it was, a vain superstition to trust outward forms while the heart is far from God (Jeremiah 7:4), God not bound to his symbols (Matthew 15:8), the form without the power (2 Timothy 3:5)

schools.summary: Phinehas's wife, hearing the news, going into travail: 'and his daughter in law, Phinehas' wife, was with child, near to be delivered: and when she heard the tidings that the ark of God was taken, and that her father in law and her husband were dead, she bowed herself and travailed; for her pains came upon her' — the shock of the news brought on her labour (the and his daughter in law, Phinehas' wife, was with child, near to be delivered, with child, the and when she heard the tidings that the ark of God was taken, and that her father in law and her husband were dead, she bowed herself and travailed; for her pains came upon her, the travail, the figure, the grief of the news bringing on the pangs, the woman overwhelmed, the truth that the tidings of the ark and the deaths brought on her travail, John 16:21, the figure of the grief that overwhelms with sudden anguish, the travail, the news of woe, vv.18); Phinehas's wife near her time (the condition, John 16:21) — the condition (with child); 'she bowed herself and travailed; for her pains came upon her' (the shock bringing on her labour, the anguish, the figure, the grief that overwhelms with sudden anguish, the providence, the travail, the naming of Ichabod to follow, vv.20-21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And his daughter in law, Phinehas' wife, was with child, near to be delivered: and when she heard the tidings that the ark of God was taken, and that her father in law and her husband were dead, she bowed herself and travailed; for her pains came upon her.
Phinehas's wife, hearing the news, going into travail
the shock of the news bringing on her labour
the grief that overwhelms with sudden anguish (John 16:21)

schools.summary: The dying woman not regarding the news of her son: 'and about the time of her death the women that stood by her said unto her, Fear not; for thou hast born a son. But she answered not, neither did she regard it' — the dying woman took no comfort in her son, her grief swallowing all (the and about the time of her death the women that stood by her said unto her, Fear not; for thou hast born a son, the comfort offered, the But she answered not, neither did she regard it, no regard, the figure, the grief so deep that even a son's birth could not comfort, the heart overwhelmed, the truth that her sorrow for the ark and the deaths left no room for joy, Genesis 35:18, the figure of the grief that swallows up every comfort, the dying woman, the comfort unregarded, vv.19); the women offering comfort of a son (the consolation, Genesis 35:18) — the comfort (thou hast born a son); 'she answered not, neither did she regard it' (the dying woman taking no comfort, the overwhelming grief, the figure, the grief that swallows up every comfort, the providence, the grief, the naming of Ichabod to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And about the time of her death the women that stood by her said unto her, Fear not; for thou hast born a son. But she answered not, neither did she regard it.
The dying woman not regarding the news of her son
the dying woman taking no comfort in her son, her grief for the ark swallowing all
the grief that swallows up every comfort (Genesis 35:18)

schools.summary: The dying woman naming her child Ichabod, the glory is departed, because the ark of God was taken: 'and she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband' — the glory that is God's presence — 'and she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken': the dying woman sets on her son the name Ichabod, 'no glory' or 'where is the glory,' because the ark, the sign of God's presence, was lost (the witnesses noting the significance — the profound truth uttered by the dying woman, that the glory of Israel was not her armies, her cities, or her wealth, but the presence of God symbolized by the ark, and that with the ark taken the glory was departed, so that her grief for the ark outweighed even the loss of husband and father-in-law, and she named her child a perpetual memorial of the calamity, 'Ichabod,' the glory gone, the witnesses noting the depth of her faith and grief, that she rightly judged the true glory of God's people to be his presence among them, and its loss the heaviest of all losses, the glory of Israel the presence of God, the ark taken the glory departed, the name a memorial of the calamity, the lesson — that the true glory and strength of God's people is the presence of God among them, and that nothing else can be their glory if he depart, 'except the LORD build the house, they labour in vain that build it,' Psalm 127:1, the truth that the worst calamity that can befall a church or a soul is the departure of God's presence, the Ichabod written over a people from whom God has withdrawn, 'woe also to them when I depart from them,' Hosea 9:12, the figure — the glory that is the presence of God, fulfilled in him who is the true glory and presence, the Word made flesh, 'we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father,' John 1:14, and whose departing leaves desolation, the truth that the loss of God's presence is the loss of all, and his presence the restoring of glory, the figure of the departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence, and its loss the heaviest of calamities, the call to prize above all the presence of God, the warning of the Ichabod written where God departs); the dying woman naming her son Ichabod, 'the glory is departed,' a perpetual memorial that Israel's glory was gone (the glory departed, Psalm 127:1) — the name (Ichabod, the glory departed); 'because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband' (the glory was the presence of God symbolized by the ark, its loss the heaviest of calamities outweighing even the deaths, the true glory of God's people his presence among them, the worst calamity his departure, the presence of God the glory, Hosea 9:12; John 1:14) — the figure (the glory that is the presence of God, fulfilled in him who is the true glory, the providence, the departed glory, the ark in the land of the Philistines to follow, ch.5) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the name Ichabod, 'the glory is departed,' signify?

sides.position: The departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence, and its loss the heaviest of calamities - the glory of Israel not her armies or wealth but the presence of God symbolized by the ark, with the ark taken the glory departed, the true glory and strength of God's people his presence among them (Psalm 127:1), the worst calamity his departure (Hosea 9:12), the glory fulfilled in the Word made flesh (John 1:14)

themes: And she named the child Ichabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband.
The dying woman naming her child Ichabod, the glory is departed, because the ark of God was taken (the crux of the departed glory, that the true glory of God's people is his presence)

the profound truth that the glory of Israel was not her armies or wealth but the presence of God symbolized by the ark, so that with the ark taken the glory was departed, her grief for the ark outweighing even the loss of husband and father

the true glory and strength of God's people the presence of God among them, the worst calamity the departure of his presence, the Ichabod over a people from whom God withdraws, the glory fulfilled in the Word made flesh (Psalm 127:1; Hosea 9:12; John 1:14)

schools.summary: The dying woman's last word, that the glory was departed for the ark was taken: 'and she said, The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken' — with her dying breath she repeated that the glory was gone (the and she said, The glory is departed from Israel, the glory departed, the for the ark of God is taken, because the ark is taken, the figure, the dying repetition that fixed the cause of the calamity, the ark the glory, the truth that she repeated the glory was gone because the ark was taken, 1 Samuel 4:21, the figure of the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all, the last word, the ark the glory, vv.21); the dying woman repeating that the glory was gone (the lament, 1 Samuel 4:21) — the glory (departed from Israel); 'for the ark of God is taken' (the ark taken the cause of the departed glory, the loss, the figure, the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all, the providence, the last word, the ark in the land of the Philistines to follow, ch.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And she said, The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken.
The dying woman's last word, that the glory was departed for the ark was taken
with her dying breath repeating that the glory was gone because the ark was taken
the heart that lays the loss of God's presence to heart above all (1 Samuel 4:21)
